doc.title: Operating activities — indirect method
doc.event: Kell Co.: net income $150,000; depreciation expense $40,000; gain on sale of equipment $10,000. During the year, accounts receivable rose $15,000, inventory fell $8,000, and accounts payable rose $12,000. Compute net cash provided by operating activities (indirect method).

variants.label: Start at net income, undo the accruals

computationPaths.narration: The indirect method is un-accrual-ing: start with net income, add back expenses that used no cash, remove gains that belong to investing, then adjust for every working-capital swing. Asset UP = cash DOWN (subtract); liability UP = cash UP (add).

steps.label: − Gain on equipment sale (belongs to investing)

memorize.body: Indirect: NI + noncash expenses (depreciation, amortization) − gains + losses (they move to investing) − ΔCurrent assets + ΔCurrent liabilities. Asset up → subtract; liability up → add.
Why add depreciation? It never left the building — it reduced income without touching cash. You're not adding cash; you're undoing a non-cash subtraction.
The GAIN isn't removed because it's fake — it's removed because the WHOLE sale proceeds report in investing; leaving the gain in operating would double-count it.

questions.prompt: NI 150,000; depreciation 40,000; gain on sale 10,000; AR +15,000; inventory −8,000; AP +12,000. Net cash from operating =

distractors.feedback: 205,000 flips two signs: the gain SUBTRACTS (its cash lives in investing) and the AR increase SUBTRACTS (those sales weren't collected). 150 + 40 − 10 − 15 + 8 + 12 = 185,000.
145,000 subtracts depreciation — but depreciation never used cash; it ADDS back. That add-back is the entire soul of the indirect method.
